nuggets.headline: Thom Yorke, the enigmatic frontman of Radiohead, once took an unusual approach to interviews during his early career.
nuggets.text: Rather than facing journalists, a "self-confessed control-freak" Yorke famously sat alone with a dictaphone and wine, interviewing himself for a Dazed magazine cover story in 1996. This candid, "wilfully schizophrenic" self-portrait emerged just before the massive success of *OK Computer*.

source.title: Splitting atoms with Thom Yorke | Dazed
source.quoteSnippet: When he was last on our cover in 1996, Yorke was riding high on the success of Radiohead’s breakthrough second album, The Bends. Back then, the self-confessed control-freak cut out the middleman entirely; he sat in a room on his own with a dictaphone and a couple of bottles of wine, got pissed and interviewed himself.

nuggets.headline: The driving force behind "Weird Fishes/Arpeggi" isn't a typical build-up, but a subtle, textural genius.
nuggets.text: While many songs use drums to delineate sections and build momentum, "Weird Fishes/Arpeggi" notably features a consistent drum groove that remains largely unchanged throughout. Radiohead instead creates its mesmerizing, evolving atmosphere through the gradual addition of layered guitar parts, crafting a "contrapuntal effect" that increases the arrangement's density.

source.title: A Harmonic Analysis of Radiohead’s “Weird Fishes/Arpeggi” – Flypaper
source.quoteSnippet: The track starts with a solid drum groove, which will stay the same throughout most of the song. This choice is quite interesting since it implies that the drums will *not* signal the subdivision of the arrangement in sections... Alternatively, the way Radiohead create momentum is done in a textural way: the density of the arrangement increases with the addition of several layers which form an interesting rhythmical and contrapuntal effect.

nuggets.headline: If the distinctive vocals and intricate layering of "Weird Fishes/Arpeggi" resonated with you, dive into the solo work of Radiohead's own Thom Yorke.
nuggets.text: Thom Yorke's solo projects, like his albums *The Eraser* or *Anima*, often continue the atmospheric and experimentally electronic leanings found in Radiohead's later work. His signature falsetto and intricate arrangements provide a natural progression for listeners who appreciate the depth of "Weird Fishes/Arpeggi," offering a similarly melancholic mood and a focus on textured soundscapes.

source.title: Thom Yorke: English musician and singer for Radiohead (1968-) | Biography, Facts, Information, Career, Wiki, Life
source.quoteSnippet: Yorke's solo work comprises mainly electronic music. His debut solo album, The Eraser, was released in 2006... Yorke released his second solo album, Tomorrow's Modern Boxes, in 2014, followed by Anima in 2019.

artistSummary: Radiohead, formed in Oxfordshire, carved a unique path from their early alternative rock days to pioneering electronic sounds. Fronted by Thom Yorke, they're celebrated for their complex, thought-provoking albums and have become one of the most influential bands of their generation.
